terms.definition: The root Java interface for groups of objects; implemented by structures like List and Set.
An ordered Collection that allows duplicates and positional access.
Resizable array implementation of List with fast random access and amortized O(1) append.
Doubly-linked list implementation of List with O(1) add/remove at ends, O(n) random access.
A Collection that contains no duplicate elements; membership-based.
Hash-table-backed Set offering expected O(1) add/contains/remove and no ordering guarantees.
terms.example: HashSet<Integer> uniq = new HashSet<>(); uniq.add(5); uniq.contains(5);

terms.definition: Associates keys to values; not a subtype of Collection.
Hash-table-backed Map providing expected O(1) get/put for well-distributed hash codes.
terms.example: HashMap<String, Integer> m = new HashMap<>(); m.put("k", 1);

terms.definition: Identifier used to retrieve a value from a Map; must obey equals/hashCode contract for hash maps.
terms.example: map.put(studentId, grade); // studentId is the key

terms.definition: The data associated with a key in a Map.
terms.example: Integer g = map.get(studentId); // g is the value

terms.definition: A key–value pair view used when iterating over a Map via entrySet.
Internal slot in a hash table where entries with the same hash index are stored.
When two distinct keys hash to the same bucket; resolved by chaining or tree bins.
terms.example: // Multiple keys stored in one bucket list/tree

terms.definition: Ratio of entries to buckets that triggers resizing to maintain performance.
An object for traversing a Collection with optional safe removal during iteration.
